metadata.summary: C4 model for hierarchical software architecture visualization: Context, Containers, Components, Code. Use Structurizr DSL, PlantUML, or Mermaid to store diagrams as code in version control.

content.title: C4 Model for Architecture Visualization
content.summary: The C4 model is a hierarchical diagramming approach for software architecture: Context (system + users + external systems), Containers (deployable units + technology choices), Components (internal structure of one container), and Code (class diagrams, usually auto-generated). Each level zooms into the previous one. Always start at Level 1; create Level 4 only from auto-generation tools. Keep each diagram to 5–10 elements — split rather than crowd.

why.p: Architecture diagrams created ad-hoc are inconsistent in abstraction level, notation, and scope, making them unusable for communication. C4 provides a shared vocabulary (Person, Software System, Container, Component) and a zoom protocol that both technical and non-technical stakeholders can navigate. Diagrams stored as code (Structurizr DSL, PlantUML, Mermaid) in version control stay synchronized with the architecture and can be generated in CI/CD.

list.item: Onboarding new contributors: Level 1 (Context) and Level 2 (Container) are mandatory.
Architecture review: create diagrams to communicate current and target state.
Documenting microservices topology: Container diagram shows services, databases, message brokers.
Recording decisions in ADRs: reference C4 diagrams for the affected components.
Sprint planning for cross-team features that touch multiple containers or external systems.
Deployment diagrams for mapping containers to cloud infrastructure.
Single-developer scripts or single-container applications where a simple README suffices.
When diagrams would be discarded immediately — invest only if they will be maintained.
Level 4 (Code) diagrams done manually — use IDE class diagram generation instead.
As a substitute for ADRs: C4 shows structure, ADRs document the decisions behind structure.
When the team has agreed on a different diagramming standard already in use — do not introduce C4 mid-project without buy-in.

section.name: level-1-system-context-diagram
section.p: Always create a System Context diagram first. It is the mandatory starting point for any architecture discussion.

rule.statement: Always create a System Context diagram first. It is the mandatory starting point for any architecture discussion. Keep it to 5–10 elements: the system, 1–3 personas, and 2–5 external systems. Do not show technology choices at this level.
rule.rationale: Without a Context diagram, stakeholders argue about scope before discussing architecture. The Context diagram aligns everyone on what the system does, who uses it, and what external systems it integrates with — all without technology details that would confuse non-technical stakeholders.

list.item: Elements: Person (user/persona), Software System (your system, in scope), External Software System (out of scope).
Key question: "What is this system and who uses it?"
Audience: everyone — technical and non-technical stakeholders.
Technology: none — no stack choices, no containers, no protocols.

section.name: level-2-container-diagram
section.p: Create a Container diagram to show the high-level technology decisions. Each container is a separately deployable or runnable unit.

rule.statement: Create a Container diagram to show the high-level technology decisions. Each container is a separately deployable or runnable unit. Show the primary technology choice and the communication protocol for each relationship.
rule.rationale: The Container diagram is the most useful diagram for software architects and developers. It shows what to deploy, what technology to use, and how the containers communicate — enough information to make infrastructure and service boundary decisions.

list.item: Elements: web apps, APIs, databases, message brokers, file storage, mobile apps.
Key question: "What are the main technology building blocks and how do they communicate?"
Audience: software architects and developers.
Label each relationship: "HTTPS/REST", "async via Kafka", "PostgreSQL protocol", "SMTP".
Maximum 5–10 containers before splitting into sub-context diagrams.

section.name: level-3-component-diagram
section.p: Create Component diagrams only for containers with complex internals that multiple teams work on.

rule.statement: Create Component diagrams only for containers with complex internals that multiple teams work on. Show the major components inside one container and their responsibilities. Do not attempt to show all containers' internals on a single diagram.
rule.rationale: Component diagrams are scoped to one container. Creating a single diagram with components from multiple containers mixes abstraction levels and becomes unreadable. One Component diagram per container, created only when that container's internals need to be communicated.

list.item: Elements: components (major named groups of code within one container).
Key question: "What are the major structural blocks inside this container?"
Audience: developers working on that specific container.
Scope: single container only; show inter-component relationships and which external containers they touch.

section.name: level-4-code-diagram
section.p: Do not create Level 4 (Code) diagrams manually. Generate them from IDE tooling or class diagram generators.

rule.statement: Do not create Level 4 (Code) diagrams manually. Generate them from IDE tooling or class diagram generators. Manual code diagrams are out of date within days of creation.
rule.rationale: Code changes constantly. Any manually maintained class diagram becomes a liability — it shows the wrong structure and misleads readers. Auto-generation from the actual code is the only sustainable approach.

list.item: Scope: UML class diagrams for a single component — rarely needed.
Use only for: complex domain models, critical algorithm internals, onboarding documentation for critical subsystems.
Tools: IDE class diagram export (IntelliJ, PyCharm), PlantUML with code annotation, Mermaid with script generation.

section.name: supplementary-diagrams
section.p: Three supplementary diagram types extend the core four levels.

rule.statement: Create a System Landscape diagram when the organization owns multiple related systems that interact. Use it as an entry point before drilling into any individual System Context diagram.
rule.rationale: Large organizations operate dozens of systems that share users and data. The System Landscape provides the "Level 0" view — all systems and their broad relationships — so stakeholders can orient themselves before zooming into one system.

list.item: Elements: multiple software systems, users, key external systems.
No technology details, no containers — this is the "birds-eye" view.
When to create: 3+ internal systems with inter-system interactions.

rule.statement: Create Dynamic diagrams to document the runtime interaction for one specific use case or user story. Use them to explain complex flows that are unclear from the static structure diagrams.
rule.rationale: Static diagrams (Context, Container, Component) show structure but not behavior. When a "place order" flow involves 7 services and a message broker, the static Container diagram cannot convey the sequence. Dynamic diagrams add time-ordered interaction without requiring full UML sequence diagram complexity.

list.item: Elements: actors (persons or systems), containers or components as boxes, numbered interaction arrows.
Numbering: explicitly number steps to show sequence.
One use case per diagram — do not combine multiple flows in one diagram.
When to create: complex asynchronous flows, distributed transactions, multi-service fan-out/fan-in.

rule.statement: Create Deployment diagrams to map containers to infrastructure. Show which containers run on which nodes (VMs, Kubernetes pods, serverless functions) and the deployment infrastructure topology. Create one diagram per environment (production, staging) when they differ.
rule.rationale: The Container diagram shows what to deploy; the Deployment diagram shows where it is deployed. Without a Deployment diagram, new team members cannot answer "what happens if this node fails?" or "which containers share a database server?".

list.item: Elements: deployment nodes (cloud regions, VMs, K8s clusters, pods), software system instances, container instances.
Show redundancy: multiple instances, availability zones, load balancers.
Label protocols and ports for firewall/security group planning.
When to create: before infrastructure provisioning, when presenting disaster recovery design.

section.name: tool-comparison
section.p: Store diagrams as code in version control using Structurizr DSL, PlantUML C4, or Mermaid C4 — never as JPEG/PNG exports from draw.io.

rule.statement: Store diagrams as code in version control using Structurizr DSL, PlantUML C4, or Mermaid C4 — never as JPEG/PNG exports from draw.io. Diagram-as-code diffs in PR review; binary image exports do not.
rule.rationale: Architecture diagrams that are not in version control diverge from the actual architecture. Diagram-as-code enables PR review of architectural changes, generation in CI/CD, and searching by element name across diagrams.

list.item: Structurizr DSL: most powerful; workspace model shared across all diagram types; supports all C4 levels + supplementary; hosted Structurizr.com or self-hosted Lite.
PlantUML C4: C4-PlantUML library; text-based; renders via PlantUML server or local JAR; good for CI generation; verbose but complete.
Mermaid C4: native support in GitHub/GitLab markdown; simplest syntax; limited to Context and Container levels; no Deployment diagram support as of 2025.
draw.io: GUI tool, good for exploratory diagramming; export DSL/XML for version control; not recommended as the source of truth for maintained diagrams.
Person: human actor — use for real users and personas; label with role name.
Software System: any application or service, in or out of scope — color-code in-scope vs external.
Container: deployable unit — include technology choice in the label (e.g. "React SPA", "Django API", "PostgreSQL 16").
Component: major logical grouping within one container — name by responsibility, not by class name.
Relationship arrows: always labeled with the protocol/technology — "HTTPS/REST", "async Kafka events", never unlabeled.
Element limit: 5–10 per diagram; more than 10 → split into sub-diagrams with a focus container/component.

antipattern.description: Mixing abstraction levels in one diagram (showing both containers and components).
antipattern.reason: Creates diagrams that are neither a Container diagram nor a Component diagram — readers cannot determine the scope and the diagram cannot be reviewed against the C4 rules for either level.

section.name: documentation-as-code-ci-cd-pattern
section.p: The documentation-as-code pattern keeps diagrams synchronized with the architecture.

list.item: Commit: diagrams in `.structurizr/` or `docs/architecture/` as DSL/puml/md files.
CI: render diagrams to PNG/SVG on each commit; publish to internal wiki or GitHub Pages.
Validation: lint DSL files for undefined elements, missing relationship labels.
PR review: diagram diffs show structural changes; require architect approval for Container-level changes.
Link in ADRs: each ADR references the Container/Component diagram snapshot from its commit SHA.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive C4 as docs-as-code with multiple coordinated agents: (1) scaffolder agent creates `docs/architecture/workspace.dsl` (Structurizr) seeded from the spec — Level 1 + Level 2 first; (2) diff agent runs on every PR, regenerates SVG/PNG via Structurizr CLI, posts visual diff as PR comment; (3) detail agent generates Level 3 (Component) for any container the PR touches, on demand; (4) deployment agent reads Terraform / Helm / Compose to produce the Deployment view; (5) reviewer agent flags violations of the "5–10 elements / one level per diagram / always label tech" guidelines. Persist DSL in repo, render to `docs/architecture/diagrams/`. Pair with `architecture-decision-records/` so each ADR can embed a Mermaid C4 snippet inline.

list.item: Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (converts diagram-driven design tasks into SDD items), a Structurizr-DSL agent (reads/writes workspace.dsl without hallucinating syntax), a deployment-view agent (reads Terraform plan + K8s manifests + docker-compose and emits Deployment view automatically), a diagram-linter agent (enforces methodology rules).

section.code: You are an architect writing Structurizr DSL. From the system summary in <spec.md>, produce a `workspace.dsl` containing only: 1. Level 1 (System Context): the system, all personas, and external systems. 2. Level 2 (Container): one container per separately-deployable unit. Add `technology` and `description` to every element and relationship. Keep ≤10 elements per view. Use `tags` for styling, no inline colors. Output only valid Structurizr DSL.

Old DSL: <paste old workspace.dsl> New DSL: <paste new workspace.dsl> Summarize architectural changes in 5 bullets max: containers added, removed, retechnified; relationships added/removed; cross-cutting impacts. Then state the ADRs that should be written or updated.

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: Tooling for rendering and managing C4 diagrams:

list.item: structurizr-cli — Render workspace.dsl → PNG/SVG/Mermaid/PlantUML, headless.
plantuml.jar + C4-PlantUML — Render PlantUML C4 diagrams in CI.
mermaid-cli (mmdc) — Render Mermaid C4 to SVG/PNG.
likec4 — Modern alternative DSL with TS-based authoring + Vite preview.
goadesign/model — C4 model in Go DSL — for Go shops.
arch-as-code — OSS suite around Structurizr.
gh-actions/structurizr-render — Pre-built GitHub Action to render on PR.
pandoc — Combine README + diagrams into single PDF for stakeholders.
claude (Anthropic CLI) — Run scaffolder + diff passes headless.
Structurizr Cloud (SaaS) — Authoritative SaaS by Simon Brown; supports DSL upload via API.
Structurizr On-Premises Lite (self-host) — Free; agents push DSL via REST API.
IcePanel (SaaS collaborative C4) — Best UX for non-technical stakeholders; export-only API for read-back.
LikeC4 (OSS / SaaS preview) — TypeScript-first DSL + interactive site; agents diff-friendly.
draw.io / diagrams.net (SaaS / OSS) — Visual editor with C4 stencils; not text-versionable — avoid for docs-as-code.
Backstage TechDocs (OSS dev portal) — Surfaces C4 diagrams alongside service catalog; agent-callable via Backstage API.
GitHub / GitLab Pages (hosting) — Render and host generated SVG/HTML diagrams from CI.
Lucidchart (SaaS) — OK for one-off; not docs-as-code; expensive for solo.
ArchiMate via Archi (OSS modeling) — Heavier enterprise modeling; rarely needed alongside C4.
OpenAPI + AsyncAPI catalogs (OSS) — Complement C4 with explicit interface contracts; agents can cross-link.
Pick one notation per repo — Structurizr DSL is the safest default for non-trivial systems; Mermaid C4 only for inline ADR snippets.
Always start with Level 1; never let an agent jump to Level 3 without Level 2 already merged.
Label every relationship with technology + intent ("HTTPS / JSON, places order"). Unlabeled arrows are noise.
Cap diagrams at 5–10 elements; if you exceed, split — methodology is correct, agents need the explicit constraint.
Render diagrams in CI (Structurizr CLI in Docker) — never let humans hand-export PNG.
Treat the DSL as code: linted, code-reviewed, semantic-versioned alongside the system.
Pair every ADR with the C4 view it touches; embed a Mermaid C4 snippet in the ADR header for context.
Pin Mermaid C4 syntax version — it is still experimental and evolves.
LLMs hallucinate Structurizr DSL keywords (e.g., invent `protocol "GraphQL"` syntax). Validate every generated DSL with `structurizr-cli validate` before merging.
Agents over-decompose into microservices on Container view because of training-data bias. Force the prompt to include team size + monolith default.
Agents skip technology labels constantly. Add a lint rule: every element/relationship without a non-empty `technology` is rejected.
Mixing levels in one diagram is a frequent agent error — explicitly say "Only Level 2 elements; do not draw classes/components."
Long DSLs blow context. Split workspaces by bounded context and let the agent edit one file at a time.
Diagram diffs as text are noisy; pair with rendered SVG hashes so reviewers see real visual changes.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: (1) initial Level 1 sign-off, (2) any new container, (3) deployment-view changes that move data across regions/clouds — these decisions usually require ADRs too.

section.name: diagram-drift-prevention
section.p: Without CI generation, the DSL goes stale within weeks. Ensure diagrams remain synchronized with architecture:

list.item: Wire CI generation as mandatory: Structurizr CLI renders on every push.
Require architecture review approval for any Container-level change in PR.
Store diagrams in same repo as code; enforce sync in code review.
Link ADRs to diagram snapshots by commit SHA — older diagrams become ADR artifacts, not current truth.

template.purpose: Structurizr DSL workspace with Context and Container levels wired up.
PlantUML C4 Context diagram template with Person, System, and External System.
